title: Spin excitation of the Heisenberg antiferromagnet with frustration: from the bounce-lattice antiferromagnet through the maple-leaf-lattice antiferromagnet to the exact-dimer system
abstract: The spin-S Heisenberg antiferromagnet on the two-dimensional lattice is investigated for S=1/2 and S=1. We consider interaction at isolated dimers ($J_{\rm d}$) and interaction bonds that form the bounce lattice ($J_{\rm b}$). For $J_{\rm d}=J_{\rm b}$, the system is reduced to the maple-leaf-lattice antiferromagnet. We primarily conduct highly parallelized numerical diagonalization to examine the spin excitation gap above the ground state for various $J_{\rm b}/J_{\rm d}$ cases. For S=1/2, we report calculations for a 42-site cluster that has not been previously treated. The S=1 case is examined for the first time for clusters up to 24 sites. Regardless of whether S=1/2 or 1, we find that the system has a gapped nature for small $J_{\rm d}/J_{\rm b}$ and becomes gapless at $J_{\rm d}/J_{\rm b}\sim 1.4$. For S=1, we also find that another gapped region appears between the gapless case at $J_{\rm d}/J_{\rm b}\sim 1.4$ and the boundary of the exact-dimer phase.
summary: This paper explores the phase transitions and spin gap behavior in frustrated 2D Heisenberg antiferromagnets. By studying the transition from the bounce-lattice to the exact-dimer system, the authors identify specific regimes where the system is gapped or gapless. The study provides new numerical data for S=1/2 clusters up to 42 sites and explores the previously less understood S=1 case. These findings help clarify the magnetic properties of frustrated lattices and relate to experimental observations in materials like Na2Mn3O7.
main_problem: The study investigates the spin excitation gap and ground-state properties of the Heisenberg antiferromagnet under varying degrees of frustration, specifically tracing the evolution from the bounce-lattice to the exact-dimer system.
main_result: For both S=1/2 and S=1, the system is gapped at low Jd/Jb and becomes gapless at Jd/Jb approximately 1.4; for S=1, an additional gapped region appears near the exact-dimer phase boundary.
method: Highly parallelized numerical diagonalization using the Lancative algorithm on clusters up to 42 sites (S=1/2) and 24 sites (S=1), with finite-size scaling analysis.
model_or_system: A 2D Heisenberg antiferromagnet with spin S=1/2 and S=1, featuring interaction bonds (Jb) forming a bounce lattice and dimer interactions (Jd) that transition the system through a maple-leaf lattice to an exact-dimer phase.
key_observables: Ground-state energy (Eg), spin excitation gap (Delta), and spin correlation functions (dimer, hexagonal, and triangular bonds).
important_parameters: Spin magnitude (S=1/2, S=1) and the interaction ratio Jd/Jb (or Jb/Jd).
main_assumptions: The study assumes periodic boundary conditions and uses finite-size clusters to extrapolate to the thermodynamic limit.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice schematic; Fig 3 shows ground-state energy per site; Fig 6 shows extrapolation analysis of the spin gap; Fig 7 shows ground-state energy as a function of Jd/Jb.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the lattice models, describes the numerical diagonalization method, presents results for S=1/2 and S=1 spin systems, and concludes with phase boundary identification and comparison to existing literature.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Form of the target system. The interaction bonds Jd and Jb are represented by broken and solid lines, respectively. Solid lines form the bounce lattice. When the broken and solid lines are equivalent, the system is reduced to the maple-leaf lattice.

FIG. 2: Finite-size clusters under the periodic boundary con- dition denoted by broken red lines: (a) unit cell of the system (N = 6) and cases (b) N = 18, (c) N = 24, (d) N = 30, (e) N = 36, and (f) N = 42.

FIG. 3: Ground-state energy per site measured in units of Jb for S = 1/2: (a) N = 18 and 36 from Ref. 4 (circles and squares linked by dotted lines) and additional results for N = 24 (blue triangles), N = 30 (green crosses), and N = 42 (red pluses). The solid line represents the energy level of the exact-dimer state. (b) System-size dependence for the cases of Jd/Jb = 0.1 (black circles) and 1.4 (red squares). Closed and open symbols correspond to the cases of rhombic and nonrhombic clusters, respectively.

FIG. 4: (a) Edge of the exact-dimer phase in the case of S = 1/2 for the N = 42 cluster. Circles show the numeri- cal diagonalization results, the horizontal broken line denotes the energy level of the exact-dimer state and the solid line is obtained by ﬁtting data outside the exact-dimer phase. (b) System-size dependence of the edge of the exact-dimer phase for ﬁnite-size clusters. Circles and squares correspond to rhombic and nonrhombic clusters, respectively. The hori- zontal broken line shows the result obtained by the coupled- cluster method in Ref. 4.
FIG. 5: Extrapolation of the spin excitation gap for S = 1/2 as functions of (a) 1/N and (b) 1/ √
FIG. 6: Fitting error bars for extrapolation analysis of the spin excitation gap for S = 1/2 using 1/N (black squares) and 1/ √

FIG. 8: Ground-state energy around the edge of the exact- dimer phase for S = 1: (a) N = 18 and (b) N = 24. Circles correspond to results from the numerical diagonalization. The horizontal broken line denotes the energy level of the exact- dimer state. The solid line is obtained by ﬁtting data outside the exact-dimer phase.
FIG. 7: Ground-state energy per site measured in units of Jb for S = 1: N = 18 (black circles) and N = 24 (blue triangles). The solid line represents the energy level of the exact-dimer state.
FIG. 9: Spin excitation gap for S = 1: N = 18 (green circles) and N = 24 (red squares). Raw data are analyzed by plotting the spin excitation gap as functions of 1/N or 1/ √
